rationale: The citing paper explicitly frames its contribution against prior bargaining research under information asymmetry and emphasizes trust/information sharing mechanisms, while the cited 2009 paper studies asymmetric-information channel negotiations with explicit communication and trustworthiness reputations. This is a strong conceptual/methodological overlap (negotiation, information asymmetry, communication, trust), so the citation is plausibly foundational rather than incidental. Given this alignment, it should be retained for full relation judgment.
